translation: Ha, Meem
commentary:
**حمٓ**

Ha Mim. This is one of the disconnected letters that opens certain Surahs in the Quran. Like other similar openings, it serves as a divine signature—a reminder that this Book is composed of the same letters the Arabs use in their everyday speech, yet they cannot produce anything remotely comparable to it.

translation: The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
**تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ**

The revelation of this Book is from Allah ﷻ, the Almighty, the All-Wise. This ayah is identical to the opening of Surah Al-Jathiyah, the surah immediately preceding Al-Ahqaf. The word تَنزِيل  emphasizes the gradual descent of the revelation—it came down piece by piece, carefully measured and timed by divine wisdom.

Allah ﷻ describes Himself here with two powerful names:  (العزيز), the Ultimate Authority who cannot be challenged or opposed, and  (الحكيم), the All-Wise whose every action and word carries profound purpose. These names frame the entire revelation—it comes from One who has absolute power and perfect wisdom.

translation: We did not create the heavens and earth and what is between them except in truth and [for] a specified term. But those who disbelieve, from that of which they are warned, are turning away
commentary:
**مَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَمَّآ أُنذِرُوا۟ مُعْرِضُونَ**

We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever lies between them except with truth and purpose, and for a specified term. Yet those who disbelieve are completely turning away from the warnings they've been given.

The phrase بِٱلْحَقِّ  indicates that creation exists with purpose, not as some random cosmic accident. Everything in the universe operates according to divine wisdom and follows precise laws. Moreover, creation has أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى —a deadline, an appointed term. Just as individual lives have their destined end, so too does this entire universe have an expiration date.

Despite this clear reality embedded in creation itself, those who disbelieve remain مُعْرِضُونَ —deliberately turning away, actively avoiding the warnings sent to them. They're not simply unaware; they're choosing to ignore what they've been told.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Have you considered that which you invoke besides Allah? Show me what they have created of the earth; or did they have partnership in [creation of] the heavens? Bring me a scripture [revealed] before this or a [remaining] trace of knowledge, if you should be truthful
commentary:
**قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ۖ ٱئْتُونِى بِكِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ هَـٰذَآ أَوْ أَثَـٰرَةٍ مِّنْ عِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ**

Tell them: Have you truly considered what you call upon besides Allah ﷻ? Show me—what have they created from the earth? Or do they have some partnership in the creation of the heavens? Bring me a book revealed before this one, or some trace of knowledge from those who came before, if you are indeed truthful.

This ayah presents a devastating logical challenge to the idol worshippers of Makkah. Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to pose three fundamental questions to those who worship false gods:

First, أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ —show me what they've created from the earth. Can you point to a single mountain, river, tree, or grain of sand that your false gods brought into existence?

Second, أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ —or do they have any share in the creation of the heavens? Can they claim even the smallest partnership in maintaining the sun, moon, or stars?

Third, if you cannot demonstrate their creative power, then bring revelation. ٱئْتُونِى بِكِتَـٰبٍ —bring me a book from before this Quran. Or bring أَثَـٰرَةٍ مِّنْ عِلْمٍ —traces of knowledge, historical documents, anything that validates your false mythologies and beliefs.

The challenge is comprehensive. Prove your gods' divinity either through evidence of their creative power or through revealed knowledge. The silence that follows this challenge is deafening.

translation: And who is more astray than he who invokes besides Allah those who will not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection, and they, of their invocation, are unaware
commentary:
**وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّن يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَن لَّا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَهُمْ عَن دُعَآئِهِمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ**

And who could be more misguided than one who calls upon others besides Allah ﷻ—beings that cannot respond to them until the Day of Resurrection, beings that are completely oblivious to their prayers?

The phrase مَن لَّا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ  reveals the tragic futility of idol worship. These false gods won't respond—not tomorrow, not next year, not until the Day of Judgment itself. And even then, their response won't be what their worshippers expect.

The phrase وَهُمْ عَن دُعَآئِهِمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ ( adds insult to injury—these false gods are غَـٰفِلُونَ (, completely unaware that anyone is even praying to them. They have no knowledge of the desperate calls being made in their name.

translation: And when the people are gathered [that Day], they [who were invoked] will be enemies to them, and they will be deniers of their worship
commentary:
**وَإِذَا حُشِرَ ٱلنَّاسُ كَانُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَكَانُوا۟ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ**

And when mankind is gathered on the Day of Judgment, these false gods will become enemies to those who worshipped them, and they will completely deny their worship.

This ayah describes a devastating scene on the Day of Resurrection. People worship various entities besides Allah ﷻ—some worship idols, others worship righteous individuals like prophets or saints. There are people who worshipped Uzair (AS), those who made 'Isa (AS) into a god, and throughout Muslim history, some have gone to extremes at the graves of righteous scholars like Abdul Qadir Jilani (may Allah have mercy on him), making prostration there instead of maintaining proper respect within Islamic boundaries.

These righteous people never asked to be worshipped. They've already passed away, and their memory has been distorted into something they never intended. On Judgment Day, when everyone is gathered and Allah ﷻ calls these worshipped entities forward, the people who prayed to them will expect them to rise as their saviors.

Instead, كَانُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَعْدَآءً —they will become their enemies. When Allah ﷻ asks 'Isa (AS), "Did you tell people to take you and your mother as gods besides Me?" he will respond, "If I had said such a thing, You would have known it." These righteous servants of Allah ﷻ will be in shock at what was done in their name.

The phrase كَـٰفِرِينَ shows they will completely reject and deny any connection to this worship. They might even be unable to process the reality: "They didn't worship us—they worshipped You! Why would they do such a thing? I called people to worship You alone, and somehow they ended up worshipping me!" The shock is too immense for them to comprehend.

translation: And when Our verses are recited to them as clear evidences, those who disbelieve say of the truth when it has come to them, "This is obvious magic
commentary:
**وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ**

And whenever Our clear and self-evident signs are recited to them, those who disbelieve say about the truth when it comes to them: "This is obviously magic!"

The word بَيِّنَـٰتٍ  describes ayahs that are crystal clear, self-evident, requiring no elaborate explanation. Yet when confronted with this clarity, the disbelievers resort to labeling it سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ —obvious magic.

This is part of an escalating pattern of accusations against the Prophet ﷺ. In previous passages, they called him insane . They accused him of being taught by others, of plagiarizing from previous scriptures. They called him a poet. Now, when they hear the Quran recited and find themselves mystified and unable to explain its power, they grasp at another label: "This must be some kind of magic. What else could it be?"

translation: Or do they say, "He has invented it?" Say, "If I have invented it, you will not possess for me [the power of protection] from Allah at all. He is most knowing of that in which you are involved. Sufficient is He as Witness between me and you, and He is the Forgiving the Merciful
commentary:
**أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ إِنِ ٱفْتَرَيْتُهُۥ فَلَا تَمْلِكُونَ لِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۖ كَفَىٰ بِهِۦ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ**

Or do they say he invented it? Tell them: If I have invented it, then you have no power to help me against Allah ﷻ. He knows best what you pour forth with your words. He is sufficient as a witness between me and you. And He is the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful.

After calling the Quran magic, they shift to another accusation: ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ —he made it up, he fabricated it. Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to respond with a powerful logical argument: If I have indeed fabricated this, فَلَا تَمْلِكُونَ لِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا—you have no control over what Allah ﷻ will do to me.

In other words, if I'm lying about Allah ﷻ, attributing words to Him that He never said, then I'm in the worst trouble imaginable—and you won't be able to help me or protect me from His punishment. I'm not seeking your protection or approval. If this is fabricated, I'm accountable to Allah ﷻ alone.

The phrase تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ  uses imagery of pouring water. The verb أَفَاضَ  literally describes water spilling and flowing, like when you pour water down a driveway and it rushes along. It's also used to describe large crowds of people pouring into a field. Here, it's describing how they let words pour from their mouths without thinking—running their mouths carelessly, letting accusation after accusation spill out: "It's magic! No, he made it up! He's crazy! He's a liar!"

Allah ﷻ says هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ —He knows better what keeps pouring from your mouths, all these baseless accusations you keep hurling.

كَفَىٰ بِهِۦ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ —He is sufficient as a witness between me and you. The case is closed. Allah ﷻ is the ultimate witness to the truth, and His testimony is all that matters.

The ayah concludes with وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ —and He is the Most Forgiving, the Most Merciful. Even after all these accusations, the door to forgiveness remains open. Even after all this hostility, His mercy is still available.

translation: Say, "I am not something original among the messengers, nor do I know what will be done with me or with you. I only follow that which is revealed to me, and I am not but a clear warner
commentary:
**قُلْ مَا كُنتُ بِدْعًا مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَمَآ أَدْرِى مَا يُفْعَلُ بِى وَلَا بِكُمْ ۖ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ**

Tell them: I am not an innovation among the messengers, and I have no idea what will be done with me or with you. I only follow what is revealed to me, and I am nothing but a clear warner.

The word بِدْعًا  refers to a new innovation, something creative that has never existed before. It comes from the same root as the divine name  (البديع)—the Originator, the One who created the heavens and the earth without any prior blueprint, purely from His own creativity.

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to declare: I'm not bringing you some revolutionary new message. Every messenger Allah ﷻ ever sent delivered the same essential teaching that I'm delivering to you. Why does this seem so strange and foreign to you?

Then comes a statement of profound humility: وَمَآ أَدْرِى مَا يُفْعَلُ بِى وَلَا بِكُمْ —and I have no idea what will be done with me or with you. This isn't the Prophet ﷺ expressing doubt about the afterlife. Rather, he's acknowledging his complete dependence on Allah ﷻ for knowledge of the future in this world. I don't know what's going to happen to me tomorrow, at the end of today, when I go to Taif, when I migrate to Madinah. I have no control over outcomes.

Consider the Prophet's life ﷺ—in Taif, he was nearly stoned to death. In Madinah, he was welcomed as a leader without ever having been there before, accepted as a ruler by people who had never met him. He truly had no idea what awaited him.

This is the greatness of all prophets—they didn't call people to accept them as rulers or to worship them. They declared themselves servants and slaves of Allah ﷻ and called people to that same servitude. The Prophet ﷺ says: I don't even have a clue what's going to happen to me. I'm compelled, I'm sanctioned, I've been charged with the responsibility of delivering whatever has been revealed to me. That's my job.

وَمَآ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ —and I am nothing but a clear warner. This is his mission, defined simply and humbly.

translation: Say, "Have you considered: if the Qur'an was from Allah, and you disbelieved in it while a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to something similar and believed while you were arrogant...?" Indeed, Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
**قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ مِثْلِهِۦ فَـَٔامَنَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ**

Tell them: Have you considered—what if this message truly is from Allah ﷻ and you disbelieve in it, while a witness from the Children of Israel has already testified to something like it, and he believed while you remained arrogant? Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing people.

This ayah poses a haunting hypothetical: What if you're wrong? What if, despite all your accusations and dismissals, this message actually is from Allah ﷻ? What if the thing you're rejecting is divine revelation?

The phrase وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ مِثْلِهِۦ —and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to something like it—is understood by most classical scholars to refer to Deuteronomy 18:18-19 in the Torah.

Let me share what these verses say: "I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. I will put My words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him. If anyone does not listen to My words that the prophet speaks in My name, I Myself will call him to account."

The parallels between Musa (AS) and Muhammad ﷺ are striking and numerous. Musa (AS) was given Law; the Prophet ﷺ was given Shariah. Musa (AS) migrated; the Prophet ﷺ made Hijrah. Musa (AS) was commanded to fight; the Prophet ﷺ engaged in defensive battles. The similarities continue extensively.

This is why Musa (AS) is the most frequently mentioned prophet in the Quran—because his story and the story of the Children of Israel have the most parallels with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and his ummah. The trials the Israelites faced mirror the trials we would face. Their relationship with their prophet reflects patterns in our relationship with our Prophet ﷺ.

So the ayah asks: A great witness from the Children of Israel—Musa (AS) himself—already testified to the coming of someone like him, فَـَٔامَنَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ —and he believed, while you became arrogant. You have the testimony of previous revelation, yet you choose arrogance over humility, rejection over acceptance.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ —Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing, the oppressing people. Guidance requires humility. When arrogance takes root in the heart, guidance cannot enter. These two cannot coexist—you must choose one or the other.

translation: And those who disbelieve say of those who believe, "If it had [truly] been good, they would not have preceded us to it." And when they are not guided by it, they will say, "This is an ancient falsehood
commentary:
**وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْ كَانَ خَيْرًا مَّا سَبَقُونَآ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ**

And those who disbelieved said to those who believed: "If this message were any good, they would not have preceded us to it." And when they are not guided by it, they will say, "This is just an ancient fabrication."

This ayah exposes a pattern that occurred with all messengers throughout history. When prophets delivered their message, they called people to leave behind greed, temptation, and injustice. The people who had the hardest time accepting this call were those doing well materially—the wealthy elite who benefited from the status quo.

Messages of justice don't resonate with those profiting from injustice. But they deeply resonate with those who are victims of injustice—the weak, the oppressed, the marginalized in society. As a result, in many cases, those who followed prophets were people without much social status.

Now the elite, when presented with the message of Islam, look at the early believers and say: لَوْ كَانَ خَيْرًا مَّا سَبَقُونَآ إِلَيْهِ —if this message were any good, this lower class would not have gotten ahead of us in accepting it. If this message had any value, we would have been the first to accept it. How can these people precede us in anything?

This reveals a class-based mentality, a supremacist mindset. They look at the skin color of believers, their geography, their economic conditions and think: How could they be more sophisticated than us in anything? How could they possibly have something to offer us? These people are less developed, less advanced, less educated. How can their religion be right?

This elitism, this arrogance, is the complete opposite of guidance. Guidance begins with humility. You must humble yourself to receive it. If arrogance occupies your heart, guidance cannot enter.

When they themselves don't commit to guidance—وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ —what's their justification? فَسَيَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ —they will say, "This is just an ancient lie, the same old stories other nations were told before."

translation: And before it was the scripture of Moses to lead and as a mercy. And this is a confirming Book in an Arabic tongue to warn those who have wronged and as good tidings to the doers of good
commentary:
**وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّسَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ**

And before this was the Book of Musa (AS)—a guide and a mercy. And this is a Book confirming it, in the Arabic tongue, to warn those who do wrong and as good news for those who excel in goodness.

The word إِمَامًا  means a leader, a guide to follow. The Torah given to Musa (AS) was كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً —a book serving as a leader and as a source of loving mercy for those who followed it.

Now comes this Quran, described as كِتَـٰبٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ —a book that confirms what came before it. It doesn't contradict the original message of Musa (AS); it affirms and validates it. But it comes لِّسَانًا عَرَبِيًّا —in an Arabic tongue, in the language of the people receiving it.

The purpose is dual: لِّيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ —to warn those who do wrong, who commit injustice against themselves and others. And وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ —as good news, as glad tidings for those who excel in goodness, for the muhsineen who strive for excellence in their worship and character.

The same message carries both warning and celebration, depending on how you receive it.

translation: Indeed, those who have said, "Our Lord is Allah," and then remained on a right course - there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ**

Indeed, those who say "Our Lord is Allah ﷻ" and then remain steadfast—there will be no fear upon them, nor will they grieve.

The phrase قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ —they said "Our Master is Allah"—represents a declaration, a commitment. But words alone are insufficient.

The word ثُمَّ —"then"—indicates sequence and effort. It's not automatic. After making this declaration, ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ —they held firm, they remained steadfast. The word استقامة  means to stand straight, to hold your course despite obstacles, to live according to what you've professed with your tongue.

This is about consistency between claim and conduct, between what you say and how you live.

For those who achieve this, فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ —there will be no fear upon them. No dangers will come their way. They won't fear what lies ahead because they know they're on the straight path.

وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ —and they will not grieve. Specifically, they won't be among those grieving on the Day of Judgment, because they'll receive what they worked for—safety, peace, acceptance.

translation: Those are the companions of Paradise, abiding eternally therein as reward for what they used to do
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ**

Those are the companions of Paradise, abiding therein eternally, as recompense for what they used to do.

The word أَصْحَـٰبُ —companions—indicates intimate familiarity. They're not just visitors to Paradise; they're its inhabitants, its residents. The word جَنَّةِ literally means a garden, but specifically a lush, dense garden where trees provide shade and protection.

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا —they will remain in it permanently, eternally. This isn't a temporary reward; it's a permanent home.

جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ —as recompense for what they used to do. This compensation is earned through consistent action—بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ , what they kept doing, habitually, continuously. It's about a lifestyle of righteousness, not isolated moments of goodness.

translation: And We have enjoined upon man, to his parents, good treatment. His mother carried him with hardship and gave birth to him with hardship, and his gestation and weaning [period] is thirty months. [He grows] until, when he reaches maturity and reaches [the age of] forty years, he says, "My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents and to work righteousness of which You will approve and make righteous for me my offspring. Indeed, I have repented to You, and indeed, I am of the Muslims
commentary:
**وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ كُرْهًا وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًا ۖ وَحَمْلُهُۥ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ ثَلَـٰثُونَ شَهْرًا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً قَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَصْلِحْ لِى فِى ذُرِّيَّتِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَإِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ**

And We have enjoined upon the human being excellence toward his parents. His mother carried him with hardship and delivered him with hardship. And his carrying and his weaning is thirty months. Until, when he reaches maturity and reaches forty years, he says: "My Lord, inspire me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents, and to do righteousness that pleases You. And make righteous for me my offspring. Indeed, I have repented to You, and indeed I am of those who submit."

The word وَصَّيْنَا  means to give strong counsel, to charge someone with an important responsibility. Allah ﷻ has charged the human being—ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ , the forgetful one—with إِحْسَـٰنًا  toward both parents. Not just basic respect, but excellence, going above and beyond.

Why this emphasis? The ayah explains by highlighting the mother's sacrifice: حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ كُرْهًا —his mother carried him in hardship. The word كُرْهًا  literally means hatred or dislike, but here it describes physical and emotional distress. Food stops tasting good. She has constant stomachaches. Her body is in a state of continuous upset, her emotions unsettled.

وَوَضَعَتْهُ كُرْهًا —and she delivered him in hardship. Labor brings pain unlike anything she's ever experienced. She's screaming, desperate for it to end. Both the carrying and the delivery are described with this same word—كُرْهًا—emphasizing the continuous difficulty.

Then comes the extended care: وَحَمْلُهُۥ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ ثَلَـٰثُونَ شَهْرًا —his carrying and his weaning is thirty months. We learned elsewhere in the Quran that nursing can continue for up to two years, and here we see the total period of carrying plus weaning can extend to thirty months—two and a half years of intensive care.

Now watch the beautiful progression in the ayah: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ —until he reaches his mature age. He's now eighteen, nineteen, twenty—a young man with his own life, his own career. And eventually, وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً —he reaches forty years of age.

Forty is recognized as the age of full maturity in Islamic tradition. In modern society, we sometimes call it the midlife crisis—the moment when the reality of mortality dawns upon you. But in Islam, it's not a crisis; it's a moment of profound reflection. Half your life is gone. Now is the time to look back and be grateful for what you've been given, to shift your focus from your own future to your children's future.

At this pivotal age, the believer makes a comprehensive supplication:

رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ —My Lord, inspire me to be grateful for Your favor. The word أَوْزِعْنِىٓ  means to give inspiration, motivation, drive. He recognizes he needs divine help even to be properly grateful. Give me the inspiration, the strength, the awareness to truly appreciate what You've given me.

ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ —which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents. At forty years old, when your parents are elderly, you recognize that every blessing in your life traces back to what Allah ﷻ did for them. None of what I have would exist if You hadn't blessed my parents, if You hadn't taken care of them. So this gratitude encompasses both yourself and your parents.

وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ —and that I may do righteous deeds that please You. Notice he doesn't say "I've already acted righteously, so You should be pleased with me." No, this is future tense—تَرْضَىٰهُ . He's saying: Give me the ability going forward to do things that earn Your pleasure. I don't want to define righteousness myself. I want to do what You find pleasing. This is humility personified.

وَأَصْلِحْ لِى فِى ذُرِّيَّتِىٓ —and make righteous for me my offspring. This is remarkable—he says "for me," not "for them." He's worried about his own accountability. When I stand before Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day, He will ask me: How did you raise your children? And if I failed in their upbringing, and they raised their children poorly, and this negative pattern continued generation after generation—I'm the one who started this chain reaction. I'm responsible.

So the request is deeply personal: For my sake, for my own benefit in the afterlife, create righteousness and goodness in my children and their children.

إِنِّى تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ —Indeed, I have turned back to You in repentance. Even at this age of maturity and reflection, even while making this beautiful supplication, he declares himself in need of repentance.

وَإِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ —and indeed I am of those who submit. This is the final statement of identity—I belong to those who have submitted themselves completely to You.

This ayah teaches us that forty is when we should be making this prayer most earnestly, especially because at this age we tend to neglect our parents the most. We're consumed with our own lives—our children, our career, our family, our savings, our house, our job. When our parents call, we say, "I can't talk right now, I'm in the middle of something, I'll call you later"—and then we forget to call back.

This pattern often starts when we're teenagers, and our parents are already tasting it if they have teenage children. But as we get older and more involved in our own lives, we have less and less time for our parents. This ayah reminds us: I need inspiration to be grateful to You and to be grateful for what You've done for my parents, because none of what I have would be possible without Your blessings upon them.

translation: Those are the ones from whom We will accept the best of what they did and overlook their misdeeds, [their being] among the companions of Paradise. [That is] the promise of truth which they had been promised
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَنَتَجَاوَزُ عَن سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ وَعْدَ ٱلصِّدْقِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ**

Those are the ones from whom We will accept the best of what they did and overlook their evil deeds, among the companions of Paradise. This is the promise of truth which they were promised.

This concluding ayah describes Allah's response to those who make the supplication in the previous ayah. The reward is extraordinary:

نَتَقَبَّلُ عَنْهُمْ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ —We will accept from them the best of what they did. Imagine your deeds throughout life are graded—some days you earned a C, some days a B, occasionally an A. But a few of your deeds, your finest moments, earned an A+. 

Allah ﷻ says He will take those A+ deeds and curve all your other deeds up to match them. It's like a merciful teacher who grades on a curve, lifting everyone's grade based on the class's best performance. Your entire record gets elevated to match your best moments. This is divine mercy on Judgment Day.

وَنَتَجَاوَزُ عَن سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ —and We will overlook their evil deeds. The word نَتَجَاوَزُ  means to pass over, to ignore, to look beyond. Your sins, your failures, your mistakes—Allah ﷻ will choose to overlook them, to pass them by.

فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ —among the companions of Paradise. This is where they'll be—not just visiting, but residing permanently among Paradise's inhabitants.

وَعْدَ ٱلصِّدْقِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ —the promise of truth which they were promised. This isn't empty wishful thinking or false hope. This is وَعْدَ ٱلصِّدْقِ —the truthful promise, the promise that will certainly be fulfilled, because it comes from the One who never breaks His word.

These opening sixteen ayahs of Surah Al-Ahqaf establish the divine origin of the Quran, refute the baseless accusations of the disbelievers, and paint a portrait of the true believer—one who declares Allah ﷻ as Lord and remains steadfast, who honors parents with excellence, who prays for inspiration to be grateful, and who will ultimately receive a reward far beyond what they could have imagined.

translation: But one who says to his parents, "Uff to you; do you promise me that I will be brought forth [from the earth] when generations before me have already passed on [into oblivion]?" while they call to Allah for help [and to their son], "Woe to you! Believe! Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth." But he says, "This is not but legends of the former people
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِى قَالَ لِوَٰلِدَيْهِ أُفٍّ لَّكُمَآ أَتَعِدَانِنِىٓ أَنْ أُخْرَجَ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلْقُرُونُ مِن قَبْلِى وَهُمَا يَسْتَغِيثَانِ ٱللَّهَ وَيْلَكَ ءَامِنْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهَ حَقٌّ فَيَقُولُ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

But there is one who says to his parents, "Uff to you both! Do you promise me that I will be brought forth when generations before me have already passed away?" While they both call upon Allah for help, [saying], "Woe to you! Believe! Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth." But he says, "This is nothing but legends of the former peoples."

In stark contrast to the righteous person of the previous ayah stands this individual—one who treats his parents with contempt and dismisses their faith. The word أُفٍّ  is the Quranic expression for frustration and dismissiveness. It's the sound someone makes when they roll their eyes, the exasperated sigh when a parent speaks. "Oh God, Mom, Dad, why don't you ever understand anything?"

This contempt reaches its peak in matters of faith. أَتَعِدَانِنِىٓ أَنْ أُخْرَجَ (Do you promise me that I will be brought forth?) he says mockingly. The parents are trying to remind their child about resurrection, about accountability before Allah ﷻ. Perhaps they see their son or daughter consumed by career and worldly success, and they gently suggest, "You have money now, you're not in debt—have you thought about going for Hajj? When was the last time you prayed?"

The response is dismissive: "Mom, I told you, don't talk to me about this religious stuff. It makes me upset. I'm just here for Eid. Let's get this over with and I'll go on my way. Let's just keep things civil, okay?"

The child's argument is one we hear echoed throughout history: وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلْقُرُونُ مِن قَبْلِى (generations before me have already passed away). "So many people have died before me, but nobody's coming out of their graves. Please stop talking about this superstition. You brought this religion from the home country, but we're in a modern age now. You can move on."

What makes this scene particularly heartbreaking is captured in the word يَسْتَغِيثَانِ (they both call for help). This verb conveys desperate pleading, the kind of cry for help someone makes when death is imminent. The parents are dying inside over their child's rejection of faith. In their desperation, they turn to Allah ﷻ and then to their child: وَيْلَكَ ءَامِنْ (Woe to you! Believe!).

Sometimes in that desperation, a mother might say things she doesn't mean: "You're such a disappointment! Why don't you listen?" But immediately she catches herself—"No, I didn't mean it. I just get overwhelmed. I don't know what to say to you anymore because you just won't listen."

They plead: إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ (Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth). There's no doubt about it. But the child's response remains unchanged: مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (This is nothing but legends of the former peoples). "Dad, you're an old-timer with old-time religious ideas. We're living in the modern age now. I've taken my philosophy courses. These are just ancient myths."

translation: Those are the ones upon whom the word has come into effect, [who will be] among nations which had passed on before them of jinn and men. Indeed, they [all] were losers
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ

Those are the ones upon whom the word has come into effect, [who will be] among nations which had passed on before them of jinn and men. Indeed, they [all] were losers.

The verdict is pronounced: حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ (the word has come into effect upon them). The judgment Allah ﷻ decreed for previous rebellious nations applies equally to them. This isn't a new phenomenon—disbelief and arrogance toward parents and faith have manifested فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم (among nations which had passed on before them).

What's remarkable is the phrase مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ . Both realms have produced those who rejected truth and chose loss. The final judgment is inevitable: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (Indeed, they were losers). Past tense—their loss is already determined, already sealed.

translation: And for all there are degrees [of reward and punishment] for what they have done, and [it is] so that He may fully compensate them for their deeds, and they will not be wronged
commentary:
وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ ۖ وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ

And for all there are degrees [of reward and punishment] for what they have done, that He may fully compensate them for their deeds, and they will not be wronged.

Allah ﷻ now establishes a fundamental principle of divine justice: وَلِكُلٍّ دَرَجَـٰتٌ (and for all there are degrees). People are not created equal in their circumstances. Some are born with sharper intellects, others with greater physical strength. Some possess artistic talents, others mathematical aptitude. Beyond individual abilities, we're born into vastly different circumstances—some in prosperous nations, others in impoverished ones. Some into wealth, others into poverty. None of this was your choice; it was Allah's decree.

But here's the crucial point: Allah ﷻ will not judge you by comparing you to others. He judges you مِّمَّا عَمِلُوا۟ (for what they have done)—based on what you were capable of versus what you actually did. Your test is unique to your circumstances, your talents, your environment—the darlun, the things you were wrapped up in.

This is true justice. A student who finds Arabic easy shouldn't be the standard for one who struggles with every pronunciation. Someone born into a family of scholars shouldn't be compared to someone who discovered Islam on their own. Allah ﷻ knows what you're capable of, and وَلِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (He may fully compensate them for their deeds)—He will give full recompense for what you did with what you had.

The ayah ends with absolute reassurance: وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (and they will not be wronged). No one will face injustice on that Day. Your unique circumstances, limitations, and abilities are all accounted for in Allah's perfect judgment.

translation: And the Day those who disbelieved are exposed to the Fire [it will be said], "You exhausted your pleasures during your worldly life and enjoyed them, so this Day you will be awarded the punishment of [extreme] humiliation because you were arrogant upon the earth without right and because you were defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّبَـٰتِكُمْ فِى حَيَاتِكُمُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهَا فَٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ

And the Day those who disbelieved are exposed to the Fire [it will be said], "You exhausted your pleasures during your worldly life and enjoyed them, so this Day you will be awarded the punishment of [extreme] humiliation because you were arrogant upon the earth without right and because you were defiantly disobedient."

The scene shifts to Judgment Day: يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (those who disbelieved are exposed to the Fire). They're presented before the Fire, and the indictment is read: أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّبَـٰتِكُمْ فِى حَيَاتِكُمُ ٱلدُّنْيَا (You exhausted your pleasures during your worldly life).

This is the person who lived by the philosophy "all we have is this life, so we should party and enjoy." Every good thing was consumed in the dunya. وَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهَا (and you enjoyed them)—you truly indulged yourself without restraint, without thought of consequence.

The verdict comes: فَٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ (so this Day you will be awarded the punishment of humiliation). Not just any punishment, but عَذَابَ ٱلْهُونِ—a humiliating, degrading torment. 

The reasons are specified: بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ (because you were arrogant upon the earth without right). Your arrogance had no justification. You walked the earth as if you owned it, as if you answered to no one. وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَفْسُقُونَ (and because you were defiantly disobedient)—you consistently engaged in corrupt behavior, in fisq, in stepping outside the boundaries Allah ﷻ set.

translation: And mention, [O Muhammad], the brother of 'Aad, when he warned his people in the [region of] al-Ahqaf - and warners had already passed on before him and after him - [saying], "Do not worship except Allah. Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day
commentary:
۞ وَٱذْكُرْ أَخَا عَادٍ إِذْ أَنذَرَ قَوْمَهُۥ بِٱلْأَحْقَافِ وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلنُّذُرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦٓ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

And mention the brother of 'Aad, when he warned his people in the Ahqaf—and warners had already passed on before him and after him—[saying], "Do not worship except Allah. Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day."

The narrative shifts to historical example. وَٱذْكُرْ أَخَا عَادٍ (And mention the brother of 'Aad)—this is Hud (AS), referred to as the brother of the nation of 'Aad. إِذْ أَنذَرَ قَوْمَهُۥ بِٱلْأَحْقَافِ (when he warned his people in the Ahqaf)—the sand dunes and heights where this powerful nation lived.

If you search for the ruins of 'Aad today, you'll find evidence of their sophisticated architecture, their advanced civilization. This was no primitive people—they were a mighty, prosperous nation.

The ayah notes: وَقَدْ خَلَتِ ٱلنُّذُرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦٓ (and warners had already passed on before him and after him). Hud (AS) wasn't the first warner, and he wouldn't be the last. The message of messengers has always been consistent.

His message was simple and direct: أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ (Do not worship except Allah). This is the core message of every prophet—pure monotheism, complete submission to Allah ﷻ alone.

And his motivation? إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day). This wasn't a threat born of malice but a warning born of genuine concern. A prophet's fear for his people's fate drives him to warn them, no matter how they respond.

translation: They said, "Have you come to delude us away from our gods? Then bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَأْفِكَنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

They said, "Have you come to delude us away from our gods? Then bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the truthful."

The people's response reveals their attitude. قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَأْفِكَنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا (They said, "Have you come to delude us away from our gods?"). The word تَأْفِكَنَا suggests turning them away through deception, making them abandon what they hold sacred.

Their challenge is bold and dismissive: فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (Then bring us what you promise us, if you should be of the truthful). "You talk about nations being destroyed if they don't follow Allah's guidance? Let's see it then. Prove you're telling the truth."

This is the age-old response of the arrogant: demanding immediate proof, immediate consequences, as if they're entitled to set the terms for divine intervention.

translation: He said, "Knowledge [of its time] is only with Allah, and I convey to you that with which I was sent; but I see you [to be] a people behaving ignorantly
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ

He said, "Knowledge [of its time] is only with Allah, and I convey to you that with which I was sent; but I see you [to be] a people behaving ignorantly."

Hud's (AS) response is measured and wise. قَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ (He said, "Knowledge is only with Allah"). The timing of Allah's decree, when His punishment will come—this knowledge belongs to Him ﷻ alone. No prophet controls when divine consequences manifest.

وَأُبَلِّغُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ (and I convey to you that with which I was sent). "My job is to communicate what I've been sent with—thoroughly, completely. That's my responsibility."

Then comes his diagnosis of their condition: وَلَـٰكِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰكُمْ قَوْمًا تَجْهَلُونَ (but I see you [to be] a people behaving ignorantly). The word تَجْهَلُونَ doesn't just mean lacking knowledge—it means being overwhelmed by emotions, by whims, letting your feelings dictate your responses rather than reason. "You get rattled easily. You refuse to have civil conversation. It's very difficult for you and me to have a calm, rational discussion with each other."

translation: And when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys, they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain!" Rather, it is that for which you were impatient: a wind, within it a painful punishment
commentary:
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عَارِضًا مُّسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا عَارِضٌ مُّمْطِرُنَا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ مَا ٱسْتَعْجَلْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ رِيحٌ فِيهَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

And when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys, they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain!" Rather, it is that for which you were impatient: a wind, within it a painful punishment.

The punishment they mocked finally arrives. فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ عَارِضًا مُّسْتَقْبِلَ أَوْدِيَتِهِمْ (And when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys)—they saw something عَارِضًا, presenting itself, greeting them, heading مُّسْتَقْبِلَ toward them from their own valleys.

Their first reaction was optimistic: قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا عَارِضٌ مُّمْطِرُنَا (they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain!"). Finally, relief from drought, blessing for their crops. "This is coming to shower blessings on us."

But Allah ﷻ corrects their assumption immediately: بَلْ هُوَ مَا ٱسْتَعْجَلْتُم بِهِۦ (Rather, it is that for which you were impatient). "No—this is exactly what you rushed toward, what you demanded to see."

What they thought was mercy was actually رِيحٌ فِيهَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (a wind, within it a painful punishment). Not rain, but a devastating tornado carrying agonizing torment.

translation: Destroying everything by command of its Lord. And they became so that nothing was seen [of them] except their dwellings. Thus do We recompense the criminal people
commentary:
تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَىْءٍۭ بِأَمْرِ رَبِّهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ لَا يُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

Destroying everything by command of its Lord. And they became so that nothing was seen [of them] except their dwellings. Thus do We recompense the criminal people.

The extent of destruction is total: تُدَمِّرُ كُلَّ شَىْءٍۭ (Destroying everything). The verb تُدَمِّرُ implies continuous, relentless crushing. We learn from other passages that this wind subdued them for seven nights and eight straight days. When a tornado passes through a town for three minutes, we see the catastrophic damage on the news. Imagine seven nights and eight days of continuous devastation in the same location.

All of this happened بِأَمْرِ رَبِّهَا (by command of its Lord). The wind itself is subject to Allah's command, acting only as He ﷻ decrees.

The aftermath: فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ لَا يُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ (And they became so that nothing was seen except their dwellings). Their bodies couldn't even be found. Only their homes remained as silent testimony to a civilization that once was.

The ayah concludes with a universal principle: كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (Thus do We recompense the criminal people). This is how Allah ﷻ deals with those who reject His guidance and persist in criminality—whether 'Aad or any other nation.

translation: And We had certainly established them in such as We have not established you, and We made for them hearing and vision and hearts. But their hearing and vision and hearts availed them not from anything [of the punishment] when they were [continually] rejecting the signs of Allah; and they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِيمَآ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِيهِ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ سَمْعًا وَأَبْصَـٰرًا وَأَفْـِٔدَةً فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَلَآ أَفْـِٔدَتُهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ إِذْ كَانُوا۟ يَجْحَدُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

And We had certainly established them in such as We have not established you, and We made for them hearing and vision and hearts. But their hearing and vision and hearts availed them not from anything [of the punishment] when they were [continually] rejecting the signs of Allah; and they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule.

This ayah drives home a sobering reality for the Quraysh. وَلَقَدْ مَكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِيمَآ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰكُمْ فِيهِ (And We had certainly established them in such as We have not established you). The nation of 'Aad enjoyed stability, power, and resources far beyond what Makkah possessed. Their civilization, military might, and sophistication exceeded anything the Quraysh could claim.

وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ سَمْعًا وَأَبْصَـٰرًا وَأَفْـِٔدَةً (And We made for them hearing and vision and hearts)—just like you, they had the faculties to perceive truth, to reason, to make sound decisions.

But here's the tragic reality: فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَلَآ أَفْـِٔدَتُهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ (But their hearing and vision and hearts availed them not from anything). All those faculties, all that power, all that sophistication—none of it benefited them in the least.

Why? إِذْ كَانُوا۟ يَجْحَدُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ (when they were [continually] rejecting the signs of Allah). They engaged in جَحْد—not just denial, but rejecting clear truth that they could see. They weren't ignorant; they were defiant.

The consequence: وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (and they were enveloped by what they used to ridicule). The very thing they mocked, the punishment they laughed about and challenged Hud (AS) to bring—it surrounded them completely, overwhelmed them entirely.

translation: And We have already destroyed what surrounds you of [those] cities, and We have diversified the signs [or verses] that perhaps they might return [from disbelief]
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا مَا حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَصَرَّفْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

And We have already destroyed what surrounds you of [many] cities, and We have diversified the signs that perhaps they might return.

The lesson becomes local and immediate: وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا مَا حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ (And We have already destroyed what surrounds you of cities). The Quraysh weren't hearing about some distant, mythical civilization. When they traveled for trade, they passed by the ruins of these destroyed towns. The evidence was visible, tangible, right in their own region.

وَصَرَّفْنَا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ (And We have diversified the signs)—Allah ﷻ has varied His approach to guidance. Sometimes He teaches through history, sometimes through reflection on creation. Sometimes He speaks about the unseen realms of heaven and hell, sometimes about the human soul itself. Sometimes He references previous revelations, other times He presents natural phenomena. The methods are diverse, the approaches multiple.

Why all this effort? لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (that perhaps they might return). The ultimate goal is always the same—that people might turn back to Him, that they might return to the truth.

translation: Then why did those they took besides Allah as deities by which to approach [Him] not aid them? But they had strayed from them. And that was their falsehood and what they were inventing
commentary:
فَلَوْلَا نَصَرَهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ قُرْبَانًا ءَالِهَةًۢ ۖ بَلْ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

Then why did those they took besides Allah as deities by which to approach [Him] not aid them? But they had strayed from them. And that was their falsehood and what they were inventing.

Now comes the devastating question about shirk: فَلَوْلَا نَصَرَهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ قُرْبَانًا ءَالِهَةًۢ (Then why did those they took besides Allah as deities by which to approach [Him] not aid them?). Where were all those false gods? Where were those supposed intermediaries?

The phrase قُرْبَانًا (by which to approach) captures the psychology of shirk. People don't typically claim their false gods are equal to Allah ﷻ. Rather, they see them as intermediaries, as means of getting closer to God. "I can't deal with Allah directly—I'm too sinful, too flawed. I need someone who can intercede for me, carry my sins, speak on my behalf."

This mentality exists in various forms across religions—whether it's certain Christian concepts of Jesus (AS), Hindu deities, saints in some Muslim communities, or even misplaced reliance on the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself. The thinking is: "I can do whatever I want, and my intermediary will make sure I'm okay. They'll vouch for me."

Islam came to destroy this entire framework. You deal with Allah ﷻ directly. No one stands between you and your Creator. The mercy of Allah ﷻ is one thing, but your deeds matter. This is the balance we must understand.

The ayah answers its own question: بَلْ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنْهُمْ (But they had strayed from them). Those supposed helpers, those gods they relied on—they vanished, abandoned them, offered no help whatsoever when destruction came.

The final verdict: وَذَٰلِكَ إِفْكُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (And that was their falsehood and what they were inventing). All of it—the gods, the intercession, the protection—was إِفْكُهُمْ, their vicious lie, their self-deception, something they invented and fabricated.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when We directed to you a few of the jinn, listening to the Qur'an. And when they attended it, they said, "Listen quietly." And when it was concluded, they went back to their people as warners
commentary:
وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَآ إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنصِتُوا۟ ۖ فَلَمَّا قُضِىَ وَلَّوْا۟ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِم مُّنذِرِينَ

And [mention, O Muhammad], when We directed to you a few of the jinn, listening to the Qur'an. And when they attended it, they said, "Listen quietly." And when it was concluded, they went back to their people as warners.

The surah takes a remarkable turn with وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَآ إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ (And when We directed to you a few of the jinn). This refers to a deeply meaningful moment in the Prophet's ﷺ life—one of his most difficult experiences.

The context: the Prophet ﷺ had gone to Ta'if, hoping that perhaps the people there would be receptive to the message since Makkah had rejected him so thoroughly. Instead, he experienced one of the most painful episodes of his mission. The leaders of Ta'if not only rejected him but incited hooligans to stone him. His blessed feet filled with blood. He could barely walk and collapsed under a tree for shade.

In that moment of exhaustion and disappointment, Angel Jibreel (AS) appeared and offered to destroy Ta'if completely—the mountains could be brought together to crush the entire city. The Prophet's ﷺ response captures his mercy: "No, perhaps their children will be believers one day."

As he made his way back from Ta'if, he stopped in the valley gardens of Nakhlah and prayed Fajr. There were only two people present—the Prophet ﷺ and his freed slave Zayd ibn Harithah (RA). The Prophet ﷺ was in a state of depression, feeling that no one would listen to his message. These people of Ta'if had rejected everything he had to offer.

But Allah ﷻ had a surprise for him: وَإِذْ صَرَفْنَآ إِلَيْكَ نَفَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (when We directed to you a few of the jinn, listening to the Qur'an). A party of jinn was passing through and overheard the recitation. The Prophet ﷺ wasn't even preaching—he was simply praying, reciting Quran in his salah.

فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنصِتُوا۟ (And when they attended it, they said, "Listen quietly"). They recognized something extraordinary and instructed each other to be silent, to listen with full attention and respect.

فَلَمَّا قُضِىَ وَلَّوْا۟ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (And when it was concluded, they went back to their people as warners). When the recitation finished, they didn't just leave impressed—they returned to their people as warners, as messengers carrying what they had heard.

This ayah contains profound comfort for the Prophet ﷺ and for us. You'll find audiences you can't even see. Your efforts aren't wasted. Sometimes when you think no one is listening, there are witnesses you're unaware of. The Prophet ﷺ was just praying, depressed, thinking his message had fallen on deaf ears—and Allah ﷻ sent an audience from an unseen realm to hear him.

This is also why in the Quran, when it mentions those who witness Fajr prayer, it speaks of both humans and jinn. It's motivation to pray longer Fajr, to recite more Quran in that prayer, because it's witnessed not just by Allah ﷻ but by audiences we cannot see.

translation: They said, "O our people, indeed we have heard a [recited] Book revealed after Moses confirming what was before it which guides to the truth and to a straight path
commentary:
**قَالُوا۟ يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا كِتَـٰبًا أُنزِلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِلَىٰ طَرِيقٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ**

The jinn begin their address to their people with يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ  - "O our people!" This form of address carries intimacy and urgency. They declare: إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا كِتَـٰبًا - "We have certainly heard a book" - emphasizing the certainty of their testimony.

They identify this book as having been revealed أُنزِلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ  - "after Musa (AS)." This is significant because it indicates these jinn had knowledge of previous scriptures and prophets. They recognized the Quran as part of a continuum of divine revelation, not something disconnected from what came before.

They describe it as مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ  - "confirming what came before it." The phrase literally means "what is between its hands" - the revelations that preceded it. The Quran doesn't contradict or replace the original messages given to previous prophets; rather, it confirms and fulfills them. This is critical: the jinn understood that the Quran validates the core message of the Torah and earlier scriptures.

Then they describe its function: يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلْحَقِّ  - "it guides to the truth" - and وَإِلَىٰ طَرِيقٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ - "and to a straight path." The Quran doesn't just contain truth; it actively guides toward truth. And it doesn't just point to any path, but to a مُّسْتَقِيمٍ  - a straight, direct path that doesn't deviate.

translation: O our people, respond to the Messenger of Allah and believe in him; Allah will forgive for you your sins and protect you from a painful punishment
commentary:
**يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ أَجِيبُوا۟ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُجِرْكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ**

The jinn continue their call with even greater urgency: يَـٰقَوْمَنَآ أَجِيبُوا۟ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ  - "O our people! Respond to the caller of Allah!" The دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ - the caller of Allah - refers to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. They recognized him not merely as a man reciting words, but as someone calling people on behalf of Allah ﷻ Himself.

وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ  - "and believe in him." This belief encompasses both the message and the messenger. The jinn understood that accepting the Quran means accepting the one whom Allah ﷻ sent to deliver it.

Then they present the benefits of responding to this call. يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ  - "He will forgive for you from your sins." The use of مِّن  - "from" - is significant. It can mean Allah ﷻ will forgive a portion of your sins, or it emphasizes the removal and separation of sins from the person. The forgiveness isn't just overlooking errors; it's actively removing the burden and consequences of wrongdoing.

وَيُجِرْكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ  - "and He will protect you from painful punishment." The word يُجِرْكُم comes from the same root as إِجَارَة  - to give protection, to shield, to provide sanctuary. Similar to how we say in Ramadan, اللَّهُمَّ أَجِرْنَا مِنَ النَّارِ  - "O Allah, protect us from the Fire." Allah ﷻ will shield those who respond to His call from the painful consequences of their past wrongs.

translation: But he who does not respond to the Caller of Allah will not cause failure [to Him] upon earth, and he will not have besides Him any protectors. Those are in manifest error
commentary:
**وَمَن لَّا يُجِبْ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَيْسَ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءُ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ**

After presenting the positive outcome of responding to Allah's ﷻ call, the jinn now address the consequence of rejection. وَمَن لَّا يُجِبْ دَاعِىَ ٱللَّهِ - "And whoever does not respond to the caller of Allah."

فَلَيْسَ بِمُعْجِزٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ  - "then he will not be able to overpower anyone in the land" or more literally, "he will not frustrate Allah's plan on earth." The word مُعْجِزٍ  means to make something fail or to render someone incapable. No one who rejects Allah's ﷻ call has the power to prevent His will from being executed. They have no power in this world to escape or overcome what Allah ﷻ has decreed.

وَلَيْسَ لَهُۥ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءُ  - "and he will not have any protective friends besides Him." When someone rejects the protection and friendship of Allah ﷻ, they imagine they can find security elsewhere - in wealth, in people, in power. But the jinn warn: there are no protective allies besides Allah ﷻ. All other sources of protection are illusory.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ  - "Those people are in clear misguidance." The word مُّبِينٍ  means obvious, manifest, clear. Their state of being lost isn't hidden or debatable. It's evident and unmistakable. These jinn, after hearing just one recitation of the Quran, understood these realities with such clarity that they could articulate them powerfully to their own people.

translation: Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and earth and did not fail in their creation, is able to give life to the dead? Yes. Indeed, He is over all things competent
commentary:
**أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَمْ يَعْىَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ**

Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses those who doubt resurrection. أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟  - "Have they not seen?" This isn't about physical sight but about reflection and realization. أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ - "that Allah is the One who created the heavens and the earth."

The next phrase addresses a critical theological point: وَلَمْ يَعْىَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ  - "and He was not exhausted by creating them." The word يَعْىَ means to become tired, worn out, or overwhelmed. This directly refutes a mistranslation and misunderstanding that had crept into earlier scriptures - the idea that God rested on the seventh day because He was tired.

The jinn who heard the Quran would have known from the revelation given to Musa (AS) about the seventh day. But in some interpretations and translations, "rested" was understood to mean God needed rest because He was tired. Allah ﷻ clarifies here: وَلَمْ يَعْىَ - He didn't become exhausted. The word "rest" in its original sense can mean to stop, but through translation into other languages, it acquired connotations of tiredness. This is one of the profound problems of translating sacred text from its original divine language into human languages - meaning shifts and can even become distorted.

Then comes the logical conclusion: بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ  - "Is He not capable of bringing the dead back to life?" The One who created the entire universe without any effort or exhaustion - is He incapable of resurrection? The question answers itself.

بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ  - "Of course! Indeed He has complete power over all things." The word قَدِيرٌ  emphasizes total, perfect ability. There is nothing outside the realm of His capability.

translation: And the Day those who disbelieved are exposed to the Fire [it will be said], "Is this not the truth?" They will say, "Yes, by our Lord." He will say, "Then taste the punishment because you used to disbelieve
commentary:
**وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا ۚ قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ**

This ayah presents a devastating scene from the Day of Judgment. وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ  - "And the day on which those who disbelieved will be brought before the Fire." The word يُعْرَضُ  means to be presented, displayed, or exposed to something.

They will be asked: أَلَيْسَ هَـٰذَا بِٱلْحَقِّ  - "Is this not real? Is this not the truth?" This is the reality they denied in the world. They dismissed resurrection as impossible, they rejected the warnings of the messengers, they mocked those who believed. Now they stand before the very thing they claimed would never happen.

قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا  - "They will say: Yes! By our Master!" Notice the irony - وَرَبِّنَا means "by our Lord" or "by our Master." Now they acknowledge His mastery and lordship. Now they're willing to swear by Him. But where was this acknowledgment when it mattered? They didn't accept His lordship and mastery in the world, but now, when it's too late, they're ready to testify to it.

قَالَ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ - "He will say: Then taste the punishment because of the disbelief you were engaged in." The command to "taste" the punishment is particularly powerful - it's not just experiencing pain but being forced to fully experience and recognize what they brought upon themselves through their persistent rejection of truth.

translation: So be patient, [O Muhammad], as were those of determination among the messengers and do not be impatient for them. It will be - on the Day they see that which they are promised - as though they had not remained [in the world] except an hour of a day. [This is] notification. And will [any] be destroyed except the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
**فَٱصْبِرْ كَمَا صَبَرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعَزْمِ مِنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ وَلَا تَسْتَعْجِل لَّهُمْ ۚ كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ مَا يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّن نَّهَارٍۭ ۚ بَلَـٰغٌ ۚ فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ**

After describing the rejection of messengers and the fate of disbelievers, Allah ﷻ now addresses His Prophet ﷺ directly with a command that would shape his mission: فَٱصْبِرْ  - "So be patient."

But this isn't a general call to patience. It's كَمَا صَبَرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعَزْمِ مِنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ  - "just as those of great resolve among the messengers were patient." The phrase أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعَزْمِ  refers to messengers of extraordinary determination and commitment. Who are they? Surah Ash-Shura identifies them for us: Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa (peace be upon them all), and Muhammad ﷺ - five messengers of tremendous resolve. Some scholars include others, but the majority agree on these five.

The Prophet ﷺ is being told: look to their examples, learn from their stories, and cultivate the same patience they demonstrated in the face of opposition and hardship. Take them as your role models in patience.

وَلَا تَسْتَعْجِل لَّهُمْ  - "And don't rush on their behalf" or "don't be hasty regarding them." This is critical. When people continuously mock and insult the message, when they reject truth with arrogance, the natural human response is to want immediate justice. The Prophet ﷺ is being told: don't pray for their immediate destruction. Don't ask for swift punishment to be brought upon them.

This connects to what happened in previous surahs. Remember when Musa (AS) faced Pharaoh's tyranny and said, "Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and harden their hearts"? That was a prayer for their destruction. Allah ﷻ is telling His Prophet ﷺ: don't do that. Don't rush to that. Why? Because if you pray against them, and Allah ﷻ accepts that prayer, it will be done immediately. There will be no chance for them to change, no opportunity for their children to become believers, no possibility for guidance to reach them later.

كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ مَا يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا سَاعَةً مِّن نَّهَارٍۭ  - "It will be as though, on the day they see what they've been promised, they didn't remain in this life except for an hour of a day." When they finally face the reality of resurrection and judgment, their entire worldly existence will feel like it lasted no more than a brief moment - just a small portion of an afternoon. All their years of denial, all their time spent in rejection, will seem like nothing more than a fleeting instant.

بَلَـٰغٌ - "This is a clear communication" or "This is a clear delivery of the message." Allah ﷻ has communicated clearly. The truth has been conveyed. The message has been delivered.

فَهَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ  - "Then will anyone be destroyed except the corrupt people?" or more literally, "Is there anyone that will be annihilated if not the nation of those who are inherently corrupt?"

The word ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ  refers to those who are rebellious, who have broken the bounds, who are corrupt in their very nature. What is the corruption here? It's not just individual sins. The corruption is hearing the message of the messenger and not taking it seriously at all.

But to understand this fully, we must recognize something important: the message isn't just the book being recited. It's being recited by the messenger. It's a combination of two things working together - the highest character embodied by the messengers, and the divine message they carry with them. Two elements validate the truth: the argument itself, the evidence, the revelation - and the character of the one delivering it. Both must be present together.

If someone witnesses both the perfect character of the Prophet ﷺ and the perfect message of the Quran, and still these two things together are not enough for them, then all you can do is be patient. Why? Because if you pray against them right now, it will be over immediately. But in the end, the ultimate destruction is reserved only for those who are inherently corrupt - those who, despite clear evidence and noble character, simply refuse to care about divine revelation.
